explorations.title: Mandelbrot Set
explorations.tutorial:
Infinite Complexity from z² + c

The Mandelbrot set is generated by the simplest possible feedback loop: take a number, square it, add a constant, and repeat. For some values of c the iteration stays bounded; for others it escapes to infinity. The boundary between these behaviors is the fractal — infinitely complex, yet defined by a single equation.

Experiments

  • Zoom into the boundary — every magnification reveals new structures.
  • Look for miniature copies of the whole set, called "satellite Mandelbrot sets."
  • The colors encode escape speed: how many iterations before |z| exceeds 2.
  • Explore the Seahorse Valley (the junction between the main bulb and the head) for spectacular spirals.
  • Each point in the Mandelbrot set corresponds to a connected Julia set — click Related to explore that connection.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens at the boundary between order and chaos?

explorations.title: Newton Fractal
explorations.tutorial:
How Newton Fractals are Computed

For each pixel z₀ in the complex plane, we iterate Newton's method for f(z) = z^n - 1. The method converges to one of the n roots of unity (equally spaced on the unit circle). We color by which root, shade by speed.

// For each pixel z:
for (let i = 0; i < maxIter; i++) {
  const fz = cpow(z, n) - 1;
  const fpz = n * cpow(z, n-1);
  const step = fz / fpz;
  z = z - damping * step;
  if (|step| < tolerance) break;
}
// Color by angle of z (which root)
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens when Newton's method meets the complex plane?

explorations.title: Peter de Jong
explorations.tutorial:
How the De Jong Attractor is Computed

Starting from an initial point, we repeatedly apply the iteration formula millions of times. Each visited point increments a counter in a density grid. Brighter areas have been visited more often.

let x = 0.1, y = 0.1;
const density = new Uint32Array(width * height);

for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
  const nx = Math.sin(a * y) - Math.cos(b * x);
  const ny = Math.sin(c * x) - Math.cos(d * y);
  x = nx;
  y = ny;

  // Map to pixel and increment density
  const px = Math.floor((x - xMin) / (xMax - xMin) * width);
  const py = Math.floor((y - yMin) / (yMax - yMin) * height);
  if (px >= 0 && px < width && py >= 0 && py < height) {
    density[py * width + px]++;
  }
}

The density buffer is then rendered with a logarithmic color map on the GPU for smooth visualization.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What patterns hide inside four numbers and two equations?

explorations.title: Logistic Map
explorations.tutorial:
How the Bifurcation Diagram is Computed

For each value of r, we iterate the logistic map many times. After discarding transient iterations (to reach the attractor), we plot the remaining x values vertically at that r position.

for (let step = 0; step < rSteps; step++) {
  const r = rMin + step * (rMax - rMin) / rSteps;
  let x = 0.5; // initial condition

  // Discard transient
  for (let i = 0; i < transient; i++) {
    x = r * x * (1 - x);
  }

  // Collect samples on the attractor
  for (let i = 0; i < samples; i++) {
    x = r * x * (1 - x);
    // Plot point at (r, x)
    points.push(r, x);
  }
}

Guided Tour

Click to zoom into notable regions of the bifurcation diagram:

Lyapunov Overlay Explained

When enabled, the colored band at the bottom shows the Lyapunov exponent λ(r). Blue regions (λ < 0) indicate stable periodic orbits. Red regions (λ > 0) indicate chaos. The boundary (λ = 0) marks where bifurcations occur.

Feigenbaum's Universality

The ratios of successive bifurcation intervals converge to δ ≈ 4.6692... and the scaling of the attractor converges to α ≈ 2.5029... These constants are universal — they appear in any system undergoing period-doubling, regardless of the specific function. This is one of the most remarkable discoveries in chaos theory.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Where exactly does a simple equation break into chaos?

explorations.title: 2D Map Bifurcation
explorations.tutorial:
How 2D Map Bifurcation Works

For each value of the swept parameter, iterate the 2D map. After discarding transient iterations, plot the x (or y) coordinate vertically. This reveals the attractor structure as the parameter changes.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What does chaos look like in two dimensions?

explorations.title: Sierpinski Triangle
explorations.tutorial:
How the Sierpinski Triangle is Computed

The chaos game is elegantly simple. Three vertices define a triangle. At each step, we randomly select one vertex and move our current point halfway toward it.

const vertices = [[0, 0], [1, 0], [0.5, Math.sqrt(3) / 2]];
let x = 0.1, y = 0.1;
const density = new Uint32Array(width * height);

for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
  const v = vertices[Math.floor(Math.random() * 3)];
  x = (x + v[0]) / 2;
  y = (y + v[1]) / 2;

  const px = Math.floor((x - xMin) / (xMax - xMin) * width);
  const py = Math.floor((y - yMin) / (yMax - yMin) * height);
  if (px >= 0 && px < width && py >= 0 && py < height) {
    density[py * width + px]++;
  }
}

The resulting pattern is a perfect Sierpinski triangle — demonstrating how randomness and a simple rule can generate precise fractal structure.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you build something infinitely complex from one simple rule?

explorations.title: Barnsley Fern
explorations.tutorial:
How the Barnsley Fern is Computed

At each step, one of four affine transformations is chosen randomly (with weighted probabilities). Each transform handles a different part of the fern: the stem, the main body, or the left/right leaflets.

let x = 0, y = 0;
const density = new Uint32Array(width * height);

for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
  const r = Math.random();
  let nx, ny;
  if (r < 0.01) {
    nx = 0;
    ny = 0.16 * y;
  } else if (r < 0.86) {
    nx = 0.85 * x + 0.04 * y;
    ny = -0.04 * x + 0.85 * y + 1.6;
  } else if (r < 0.93) {
    nx = 0.2 * x - 0.26 * y;
    ny = 0.23 * x + 0.22 * y + 1.6;
  } else {
    nx = -0.15 * x + 0.28 * y;
    ny = 0.26 * x + 0.24 * y + 0.44;
  }
  x = nx; y = ny;
  // ... bin into density grid
}

The f2 transform (85% probability) generates the self-similar copies of the whole fern, giving it its recursive structure.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does pure randomness grow a perfect fern?

explorations.title: Henon Map
explorations.tutorial:
How the Hénon Map is Computed

The Hénon map is one of the simplest 2D maps that produces chaotic behavior. We iterate the map and bin the visited points into a density histogram.

let x = 0.1, y = 0.1;
const density = new Uint32Array(width * height);

for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
  const nx = 1 - a * x * x + y;
  const ny = b * x;
  x = nx;
  y = ny;

  const px = Math.floor((x - xMin) / (xMax - xMin) * width);
  const py = Math.floor((y - yMin) / (yMax - yMin) * height);
  if (px >= 0 && px < width && py >= 0 && py < height) {
    density[py * width + px]++;
  }
}

The quadratic term (−ax²) creates the folding that leads to chaos, while the linear term (by) provides the stretching.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can a simple map create structure that never repeats?

explorations.title: Affine IFS
explorations.tutorial:
How the Chaos Game Works

The random iteration algorithm (chaos game) generates the attractor of an IFS:

let x = 0, y = 0;
const density = new Uint32Array(width * height);

for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
  // Choose a transform with probability p_i
  const r = Math.random();
  let cumulative = 0, idx = 0;
  for (let j = 0; j < transforms.length; j++) {
    cumulative += transforms[j].p;
    if (r <= cumulative) { idx = j; break; }
  }

  // Apply affine transform
  const t = transforms[idx];
  const nx = t.a * x + t.b * y + t.e;
  const ny = t.c * x + t.d * y + t.f;
  x = nx; y = ny;

  // Skip first 100 iterations (transient)
  if (i < 100) continue;

  // Bin into density grid
  const px = Math.floor((x - xMin) / (xMax - xMin) * width);
  const py = Math.floor((y - yMin) / (yMax - yMin) * height);
  if (px >= 0 && px < width && py >= 0 && py < height) {
    density[py * width + px]++;
  }
}

Probability Weights & Density

The probability pi assigned to each transform controls how often the orbit visits the region mapped by that transform. Higher pi means more points land there, producing brighter density. For a uniform visual, set pi proportional to |det(Ai)| (the area contraction factor).

Self-Similarity

The attractor A satisfies A = f1(A) \u222A f2(A) \u222A \u2026 \u222A fn(A). Each transform maps the whole attractor onto a smaller copy of itself, producing the characteristic self-similar structure of fractals like the Barnsley Fern, Sierpinski Triangle, and many others.

Transform Format

Each line in the text box defines one transform:
a b c d e f p
where the 2\u00D72 matrix is [[a, b], [c, d]], the translation is (e, f), and p is the selection probability.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How many transformations does it take to build a fractal?

explorations.title: Custom Iterator
explorations.formulaShort: x\' = f(x, y, a, b, c, d)
explorations.formula: x\' = f(x, y, a, b, c, d)
explorations.tutorial:
How Custom Iterators Work

Your expression is parsed into an AST (abstract syntax tree), validated for safety, and compiled to JavaScript (CPU modes) or GLSL (GPU mode).

// Your expression "sin(a*y) + c*cos(a*x)" compiles to:
// Math.sin(a * y) + c * Math.cos(a * x)

// In GPU mode, "z^2 + c" compiles to GLSL:
// csquare(z) + c
// where csquare is a vec2 complex multiply

Try the presets to see classic attractors, then modify the expressions to discover new ones!
explorations.teaserQuestion: What fractal is hiding in your equation?

explorations.title: L-System
explorations.tutorial:
How L-Systems Work

Starting from an axiom string, each iteration replaces characters according to production rules. The final string is interpreted as turtle graphics:

// F = move forward and draw
// + = turn left by angle
// - = turn right by angle
// [ = save position & angle (push)
// ] = restore position & angle (pop)
// Other chars: variables (no drawing)

// Example: Koch curve
// Axiom: F
// Rule: F → F+F-F-F+F
// Angle: 90°
// After 2 iterations:
// F+F-F-F+F + F+F-F-F+F - ...
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can a grammar grow a tree?

explorations.title: Mandelbrot \u2194 Logistic 3D
explorations.tutorial:
The Conjugacy c = r(2\u2212r)/4

Consider the logistic map f(x) = rx(1\u2212x) and the quadratic map g(z) = z\u00b2 + c. Define the change of variables:

x = 1/2 \u2212 z/r
c = r(2 \u2212 r)/4

Under this substitution, one iteration of the logistic map is equivalent to one iteration of the quadratic map. This is a topological conjugacy: the two systems have identical orbit structures.

As r sweeps from 0 to 4, c sweeps from 0 to \u22122 along the real axis. The period-doubling cascade at r \u2248 3, 3.449, 3.544... maps to the sequence of bulbs along the real axis of the Mandelbrot set.

// Conjugacy mapping
function logisticR_to_mandelbrotC(r) {
  return r * (2 - r) / 4;
}
// r=3 (first bifurcation) \u2192 c = 3(2\u22123)/4 = \u22120.75
// r=4 (full chaos)        \u2192 c = 4(2\u22124)/4 = \u22122.0

In this visualization, the Mandelbrot set is rendered as a floor plane. The bifurcation diagram stands vertically on the z=0 plane, directly above the real axis of the Mandelbrot set, making the conjugacy visible in 3D.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What does the bridge between order and chaos look like in 3D?

explorations.title: Julia Set
explorations.tutorial:
How Julia Sets are Computed

Each pixel maps to a starting complex number z0. We iterate z = z² + c using a fixed value of c and count how many iterations before |z| exceeds 2 (the escape radius). Pixels that never escape are colored black (in the filled Julia set).

// c is a fixed parameter (e.g. c = -0.4 + 0.6i)
const c_re = -0.4, c_im = 0.6;

// For each pixel (px, py) on screen:
let z_re = xMin + px * (xMax - xMin) / width;
let z_im = yMin + py * (yMax - yMin) / height;

for (let i = 0; i < maxIter; i++) {
  const z_re2 = z_re * z_re;
  const z_im2 = z_im * z_im;
  if (z_re2 + z_im2 > 4) {
    // Escaped — color based on i
    break;
  }
  z_im = 2 * z_re * z_im + c_im;
  z_re = z_re2 - z_im2 + c_re;
}

Connection to the Mandelbrot Set

The Mandelbrot set is a "map" of all Julia sets. Pick any point c in the complex plane:

  • If c is inside the Mandelbrot set, its Julia set is connected (one piece).
  • If c is outside the Mandelbrot set, its Julia set is a Cantor dust (totally disconnected).
  • The most beautiful Julia sets come from c values near the boundary of the Mandelbrot set.

Try the presets to see famous examples, or adjust cre and cim to explore on your own.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does one tiny number reshape an entire fractal?

explorations.title: Kleinian Group
explorations.tutorial:
How Kleinian Limit Sets are Computed

The algorithm depends on the type of group:

Circle Inversions (Apollonian Gasket)

For each pixel, we repeatedly invert the point through a configuration of circles. A circle inversion maps a point z through a circle with center c and radius r:

// Circle inversion
function invert(z, center, r) {
  const dz = z - center;
  return center + (r*r / |dz|^2) * dz;
}

// For each pixel z:
for (let i = 0; i < maxIter; i++) {
  // Find a circle whose interior contains z
  // and invert through it
  if (inside(circle_k)) {
    z = invert(z, center_k, radius_k);
  } else {
    break; // z is in the fundamental domain
  }
}

M\u00F6bius Generators (Maskit Slice)

Given two generators A and B (and their inverses A\u207B\u00B9, B\u207B\u00B9), we apply whichever transform pulls the point closest to the limit set:

// Mobius transform: z -> (a*z + b) / (c*z + d)
function mobius(z, a, b, c, d) {
  return (a*z + b) / (c*z + d);
}

// For each pixel, iterate:
for (let i = 0; i < maxIter; i++) {
  // Apply A, A^-1, B, B^-1
  // Pick the one that minimizes |Im(z)|
  z = bestTransform(z);
}

Pixels are colored by iteration depth and orbit trap distance \u2014 how close the orbit passes to the limit set.

PSL(2,\u2102) and Discreteness

The group of all M\u00F6bius transforms with ad\u2212bc=1 is PSL(2,\u2102). A Kleinian group is a discrete subgroup. Discreteness means orbits don\u2019t fill space \u2014 they accumulate on a fractal limit set. The complement of the limit set is the ordinary set (or domain of discontinuity), where the group acts properly discontinuously.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What lives at the boundary of infinite reflections?

explorations.title: Coupled Systems
explorations.tutorial:
What Coupling Does

Two identical chaotic systems with zero coupling (\u03B5=0) produce completely different trajectories from different initial conditions \u2014 that's sensitivity to initial conditions.

With sufficient coupling, something remarkable happens: the two systems synchronize. Despite being chaotic, they lock onto the same trajectory. In the phase portrait, independent systems fill a 2D region; synchronized systems collapse to the diagonal.

Try this: start with \u03B5=0, then slowly increase it. Watch the phase portrait transition from a filled square to a line.

// Additive coupling
xA_next = f(xA) + epsilon * (xB - xA);
xB_next = f(xB) + epsilon * (xA - xB);
// When epsilon is large enough,
// xA converges to xB despite chaos
explorations.overview:
What happens when two chaotic systems talk to each other?

Each system on its own is unpredictable \u2014 tiny differences in starting conditions lead to wildly different outcomes. But connect them with even a weak link, and something remarkable emerges: synchronization. Two chaotic systems can lock onto the same trajectory, creating order from disorder.

This isn't just mathematics. Fireflies blinking in unison, neurons firing together in the brain, power grids staying in phase \u2014 these are all coupled chaotic systems finding synchronization.

System A H\u00E9non map
\u2194 coupling \u03B5
System B H\u00E9non map
Phase portrait \u2014 plots xA vs xB. If they synchronize, points collapse onto the diagonal.
Coupling \u03B5 \u2014 how strongly the systems influence each other. \u03B5=0 means independent; larger \u03B5 means tighter connection.
Synchronization \u2014 when coupled chaotic systems converge to the same trajectory despite starting apart.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens when oscillators talk to each other?

explorations.title: Double Pendulum Chaos
explorations.tutorial:
How To Explore Chaos

Use the preset selector to compare nearly identical chaotic starts. Then drag either bob to create your own initial condition and press Start.

  • Drag interaction: pull bob 1 or bob 2 directly on the canvas.
  • Phase portrait: a tight loop means regular motion; a broad cloud indicates chaos.
  • Damping: increase damping to suppress chaotic wandering.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can two nearly identical starts diverge completely?

explorations.title: Firefly Synchrony
explorations.tutorial:
How To Use This Demo

  • Fly Count: increase population size to test large-group behavior.
  • Frequency Spread: widen the natural-frequency peak to make locking harder.
  • Coupling: increase K to drive alignment.
  • Strip Chart: each colored track shows one fly phase over time.
explorations.overview: Fireflies are modeled as Kuramoto-like phase oscillators with tunable natural-frequency spread and coupling strength. When coupling exceeds a critical threshold the population locks into synchronized flashing \u2014 a phase transition visible in the order-parameter strip chart and the collective blink pattern.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How strong must coupling be before a crowd blinks together?

explorations.title: Coupled Metronomes
explorations.tutorial:
What To Try

  • Escapement: the spring drive that keeps each metronome ticking. Set to 0 and watch oscillations die.
  • Bob Mass / Platform Mass: heavy bobs on a light platform create strong coupling.
  • Energy chart: watch the drive inject energy and the total reach steady state.
  • In-Phase: strong coupling and low mismatch produce alignment.
  • Mismatch: broaden natural frequencies to break lock.
explorations.overview: Spring-driven metronomes sit on a shared rolling platform, exchanging energy through the platform\u2019s motion. With sufficient coupling and low frequency mismatch, the metronomes spontaneously synchronize \u2014 a mechanical demonstration of the same phase-locking phenomenon seen in fireflies and power grids.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can a moving platform make independent clocks agree?

explorations.title: Lorenz Attractor Lab
explorations.formulaShort: x' = sigma(y-x), y' = x(rho-z)-y, z' = xy-beta z
explorations.formula: x' = sigma(y-x), y' = x(rho-z)-y, z' = xy-beta z
explorations.tutorial:
The Butterfly That Changed Physics

In 1963, Edward Lorenz discovered that a simple system of three equations — modeling atmospheric convection — produced motion that never repeated and was exquisitely sensitive to initial conditions. Two trajectories starting 0.000001 apart would eventually trace completely different paths. This is deterministic chaos: the equations are exact, but prediction is impossible.

The twin trajectories (cyan and pink) start nearly identically. Watch them track together, then suddenly diverge — the "butterfly effect" in action.

Experiments

  • Watch the twin trajectories — how long before they separate?
  • Sweep ρ from 1 to 28: stable point → two fixed points → chaos.
  • Edit the equations slightly — does the butterfly survive?
  • The Poincaré section shows how the attractor's cross-section changes with parameters.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What tiny change flips the future?

explorations.title: Perceptron Bifurcation
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Bifurcation Diagram

The x-axis is learning rate η. For each η we iterate gradient descent from an initial weight and plot the last N values of σ(w) on the y-axis.

  • Single dot → GD converges to a fixed point
  • Two dots → period-2 oscillation (weight bounces between two values)
  • Cascade → period 4, 8, 16… the Feigenbaum route to chaos
  • Dense band → chaos — the weight never settles

The left panel shows the classic logistic map r·x(1−x) for comparison. The dashed line marks the target value; the vertical dashed line marks the analytically predicted first bifurcation at η1.
explorations.overview:
Gradient descent on even the simplest neural network \u2014 a single
perceptron \u2014 is a 1D iterated map. The sigmoid derivative \u03C3(1\u2212\u03C3) is the logistic
map\u2019s nonlinearity x(1\u2212x), so Feigenbaum universality guarantees the same
period-doubling cascade. This exploration renders that cascade side-by-side with
the logistic map to make the connection visceral.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Is gradient descent a chaotic system in disguise?

explorations.title: NN Bifurcation
explorations.tutorial:
How to Use

  • Left panel (top): Interactive network topology. Click a node to observe that neuron’s mean activation, or an edge to observe that weight value.
  • Left panel (bottom): Training data scatter plot (colored by class).
  • Center: Bifurcation diagram — η on x-axis, selected element on y-axis. Each vertical slice is one training run from identical initial weights.
  • Right: Readout on hover — η, detected period, and orbit trace.

Increase Hidden Layers and Neurons/Layer to see how depth and width change the bifurcation structure. Try clicking different weights — each has its own onset of chaos.
explorations.overview:
Full-batch gradient descent on any fixed-architecture, fixed-data MLP is a
deterministic dynamical system. The single-perceptron case is equivalent to the logistic
map. This exploration generalises that result: as you add hidden layers and neurons, each
weight and activation still undergoes period-doubling — but the critical η shifts and
the cascades interleave in complex ways.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Does the route to chaos survive in deeper networks?

explorations.title: Fluid Dynamics
explorations.tutorial:
How This Simulation Works

Click and drag to inject velocity and dye into the fluid. The simulation runs the Stable Fluids algorithm (Jos Stam, 1999) entirely on the GPU.

Each timestep has four stages that you can toggle independently:

// 1. Advection: trace backward, copy
pos = currentPos - dt * velocity(currentPos);
newValue = texture(field, pos);

// 2. Diffusion: Jacobi iteration
// Solves (I - dt*visc*Laplacian) * v_new = v_old
for (let i = 0; i < 20; i++)
  v = (vL + vR + vT + vB + alpha * v_old) / (4 + alpha);

// 3. Divergence + Pressure solve
div = (vR - vL + vT - vB) / 2;
for (let i = 0; i < 20; i++)
  p = (pL + pR + pT + pB - div) / 4;

// 4. Projection: subtract pressure gradient
v -= 0.5 * gradient(pressure);

Rayleigh-B\u00E9nard mode: Heat rises from the bottom. At low heat, steady convection cells form. Increase buoyancy to see the transition to turbulence \u2014 the same transition Lorenz studied when he discovered the butterfly attractor.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you see turbulence emerge from smooth flow?

explorations.title: Thermal Diffusion
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left panel shows a cross-section of the material — surface at top, depth increasing downward. Color encodes temperature: red = hot, blue = cold, dark = near zero.

The center panel is a space-time diagram: horizontal axis is time, vertical is depth. You can see the diagonal wavefronts sweeping downward and fading — the signature of a damped traveling wave.

The right panel shows the instantaneous depth profile T(x) at the current moment, with the exponential envelope ±T₀ e−x/δ drawn as dashed curves.

Things to try

  • Increase ω — the oscillation speeds up and the penetration depth shrinks.
  • Increase α — heat diffuses faster, waves reach deeper.
  • Watch the phase lag: when the surface is at peak temperature, where is the peak underground?
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does heat know where to flow?

explorations.title: Lissajous Figures
explorations.tutorial:
How Lissajous Figures Work

The curve is generated by sampling t from 0 to 2π and plotting:

for (let i = 0; i <= steps; i++) {
  const t = (i / steps) * 2 * Math.PI;
  const x = A * Math.sin(a * t + delta);
  const y = A * Math.sin(b * t);
  // draw (x, y)
}

Integer frequency ratios produce closed curves with a fixed number of lobes. Try a=3, b=2 for a classic "figure-8" variant, or a=5, b=4 for a more complex knot.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What shapes emerge when two vibrations meet?

explorations.title: Fourier Synthesis
explorations.tutorial:
Building Any Shape from Sine Waves

Joseph Fourier discovered something astonishing: any periodic signal can be built by adding up sine waves. A square wave? That's an infinite sum of odd harmonics. The more harmonics you add, the sharper the corners become.

let sum = 0;
for (let k = 1; k <= n; k += 2) {
  sum += (1 / k) * Math.sin(k * omega * t);
}
y = sum * amplitude * (4 / Math.PI);

Experiments

  • Start with 1 harmonic — just a smooth sine wave.
  • Add harmonics one by one and watch the wave deform toward a square.
  • At 15 harmonics, notice the small overshoot at the corners — this is the Gibbs phenomenon, and it never disappears, even with infinite harmonics.
  • The faint curves show each harmonic's individual contribution to the sum.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you build any shape from pure sine waves?

explorations.title: Euler's Formula
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

A point rotates around the unit circle at angular velocity ω. Its position at time t is:

const theta = omega * t;
const x = Math.cos(theta); // Real part
const y = Math.sin(theta); // Imaginary part

The dashed projections show how the x-coordinate traces cos(θ) and the y-coordinate traces sin(θ). Together they demonstrate that circular motion and sinusoidal oscillation are two views of the same phenomenon.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does going in circles create waves?

explorations.title: Phase Space
explorations.tutorial:
How the Phase Portrait is Drawn

We compute position and velocity analytically for a damped harmonic oscillator:

const x = A * Math.exp(-b * t) * Math.cos(omega * t);
const v = A * Math.exp(-b * t) * (
  -b * Math.cos(omega * t) - omega * Math.sin(omega * t)
);

The top panel shows x(t); the bottom panel plots v vs x. Increase damping to watch the ellipse collapse into a spiral.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you see the future of a system in a single picture?

explorations.title: Simple Harmonic Motion
explorations.tutorial:
The Simplest Oscillation

Every vibration in nature — a guitar string, a pendulum, the atoms in a crystal — begins with the same equation: a restoring force proportional to displacement. The result is simple harmonic motion, a pure sinusoidal oscillation.

This is the atom of periodic motion. Fourier showed that any periodic signal can be decomposed into a sum of these simple oscillations.

const omega = Math.sqrt(k / m);
const x = A * Math.cos(omega * t + phi);

Experiments

  • Increase stiffness (k) and watch the oscillation speed up — stiffer springs vibrate faster.
  • Increase mass (m) and watch it slow down — heavier objects are harder to accelerate.
  • Adjust the phase to shift the starting point — this is the difference between sine and cosine.
  • The derived frequency ω = √(k/m) is shown below the sliders.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does everything vibrate like a spring?

explorations.title: Sine & Cosine Explorer
explorations.tutorial:
The Shape of Oscillation

Sine and cosine aren't just abstract curves — they are the shadow of circular motion. Imagine a point moving around a circle: its vertical shadow traces sin(t), its horizontal shadow traces cos(t). Every wave, every vibration, every oscillation is built from these two shapes.

const ySin = A * Math.sin(omega * t + phi);
const yCos = A * Math.cos(omega * t);

Experiments

  • Amplitude stretches the wave vertically — taller peaks, deeper troughs, more energy.
  • Frequency compresses it horizontally — more cycles in the same time, higher pitch.
  • Phase shifts the wave in time. Set φ = π/2 and sine becomes cosine — they are the same shape, offset by a quarter cycle.
  • Turn on the cosine overlay and press Animate to see both functions dance together, always a quarter-cycle apart.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What makes sine and cosine the most fundamental curves?

explorations.title: Complex Spiral
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

The trail plots z(t) on the complex plane as time advances:

const r = Math.exp(sigma * t);
const x = r * Math.cos(omega * t);
const y = r * Math.sin(omega * t);

A fading trail shows the most recent positions. The unit circle is drawn for reference, and envelope circles at eσt show how the magnitude grows or shrinks.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What does multiplication look like in the complex plane?

explorations.title: Archimedean Spiral
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

The polar equation is converted to Cartesian coordinates for drawing:

const r = a + b * theta;
const x = r * Math.cos(theta);
const y = r * Math.sin(theta);

Construction lines show the current radius and angle. Adjust growthRate (b) to change the spacing, startOffset (a) to push the spiral away from the center, and turns to control how many revolutions are drawn.
explorations.overview: The Archimedean spiral r = a + b\u03B8 is the simplest spiral: turns are evenly spaced because the radius grows linearly with angle. Adjust the growth rate b to widen or tighten the spacing, and watch how the construction lines trace the relationship between polar coordinates and the resulting curve.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What curve does a point make moving steadily along a spinning arm?

explorations.title: Roots of Unity
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

The n roots are plotted on the unit circle in the complex plane:

for (let k = 0; k < n; k++) {
  const angle = (2 * Math.PI * k) / n;
  const x = Math.cos(angle);
  const y = Math.sin(angle);
}

When the polygon overlay is enabled, edges connect successive roots to form a regular polygon. Labels show each root’s index and coordinates. Use the zoom slider to scale the view.
explorations.overview: The n-th roots of unity are the n complex solutions of z\u207F = 1, spaced equally around the unit circle. They form a regular polygon whose vertices encode deep connections between algebra, geometry, and the discrete Fourier transform. Toggle the polygon overlay to see the symmetry directly.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why do the solutions of z\u207F = 1 form a perfect polygon?

explorations.title: Cycloid
explorations.formulaShort: x = r(t − sin t), y = r(1 − cos t)
explorations.formula: x = r(t − sin t), y = r(1 − cos t)
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

The rolling circle animation shows the wheel moving right as the pen traces the curve:

const x = r * (t - d * Math.sin(t));
const y = r * (1 - d * Math.cos(t));

The wheel rolls without slipping: it advances by r·Δt for each radian of rotation. The pen point (shown as a dot) draws the trail behind it. Adjust penRatio to switch between curtate, standard, and prolate cycloids.
explorations.overview: A cycloid is the curve traced by a point on the rim of a circle rolling along a straight line. It is simultaneously the brachistochrone (fastest descent under gravity) and the tautochrone (equal descent time regardless of starting position). Adjust the pen ratio to explore curtate and prolate variants.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What path does a point on a rolling wheel trace?

explorations.title: Rose Curves
explorations.tutorial:
Drawing Rose Curves

The polar equation is sampled over θ and converted to Cartesian coordinates:

for (let i = 0; i <= steps; i++) {
  const theta = (i / steps) * maxTheta;
  const r = A * Math.cos(k * theta);
  const x = r * Math.cos(theta);
  const y = r * Math.sin(theta);
  // plot (x, y)
}

Try integer values of k first to see symmetric petals. Then sweep k slowly between integers to watch the curve morph continuously.
explorations.overview: Rose curves are polar flowers defined by r = A\u00B7cos(k\u03B8). The number of petals depends on whether k is even, odd, or fractional. Animate k through non-integer values to watch petals merge and split, revealing the connection between polar symmetry and rational versus irrational frequency ratios.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How many petals does sin(n\u03B8) draw?

explorations.title: Epitrochoids (Spirograph)
explorations.tutorial:
How Spirograph Curves Work

The rolling circle traces the curve as parameter t increases:

const R = fixedRadius, r = rollingRadius, d = penOffset;
for (let i = 0; i <= steps; i++) {
  const t = (i / steps) * maxT;
  const x = (R + r) * Math.cos(t) - d * Math.cos((R + r) * t / r);
  const y = (R + r) * Math.sin(t) - d * Math.sin((R + r) * t / r);
}

The curve closes when R/r is rational. Try R = 5, r = 3 for classic five-lobed patterns, or set r to an irrational fraction of R for space-filling paths.
explorations.overview: An epitrochoid is the curve traced by a pen on a small circle rolling around the outside of a larger circle \u2014 the mathematics behind Spirograph toys. Rational radius ratios produce closed patterns that repeat after a finite number of loops; irrational ratios generate space-filling paths that never exactly close.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens when a circle rolls around another circle?

explorations.title: Taylor Series
explorations.tutorial:
Infinite Polynomials That Capture Functions

A Taylor series is a polynomial of infinite degree that matches a function perfectly — at least near one point. Each additional term captures one more derivative, extending the approximation a little farther.

For sin(x), the first term is just the line y = x. Add the cubic correction x³/6 and the curve bends. Add more terms and the polynomial hugs the sine wave over an ever-wider range.

function taylorSin(x, N) {
  let sum = 0;
  for (let n = 0; n < N; n++) {
    sum += Math.pow(-1, n) * Math.pow(x, 2*n+1) / factorial(2*n+1);
  }
  return sum;
}

Experiments

  • Start with 1 term: just a line. It matches near the origin and nowhere else.
  • Add terms one at a time — watch the polynomial extend its reach.
  • Zoom out (increase x range) to see where the approximation breaks down.
  • Switch to ex — its Taylor series converges everywhere, but slowly for large x.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you rebuild any function from its derivatives at one point?

explorations.title: Damped Oscillation
explorations.tutorial:
Regimes of Damping

The character of the motion depends entirely on the discriminant ω0² − b²:

const disc = omega0 * omega0 - b * b;
if (disc > 0) {
  // Underdamped: oscillate inside envelope (x(0)=A, x'(0)=0)
  const omegaD = Math.sqrt(disc);
  x = A * Math.exp(-b * t) * (Math.cos(omegaD * t) + (b / omegaD) * Math.sin(omegaD * t));
} else if (disc === 0) {
  // Critical: fastest non-oscillatory decay
  x = A * (1 + b * t) * Math.exp(-b * t);
} else {
  // Overdamped: two decaying exponentials (x(0)=A, x'(0)=0)
  const s = Math.sqrt(-disc);
  const s1 = -b + s;
  const s2 = -b - s;
  x = A / (2 * s) * ((b + s) * Math.exp(s1 * t) + (s - b) * Math.exp(s2 * t));
}

Drag the damping slider to transition between regimes and watch the regime label update in real time.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does friction steal energy from a pendulum?

explorations.title: Trig Identities (Unit Circle)
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

A point P sits on the unit circle at angle θ. Its coordinates are:

const x = Math.cos(theta);
const y = Math.sin(theta);

In Pythagorean mode, a right triangle from the origin to P shows the legs cosθ and sinθ with hypotenuse = 1. In Tangent mode the tangent line at (1, 0) extends to meet the radius line, giving a segment of length tanθ. The Reciprocal mode draws secθ and cscθ as full segments from the origin through P to the axes.
explorations.overview: Fundamental trigonometric identities are visualized as geometric relationships on the unit circle. Move the point P around the circle to see the Pythagorean, tangent, and reciprocal identities as actual line-segment lengths, making abstract algebra concrete and intuitive.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you see a trig identity?

explorations.title: Wave Identities
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

The top half draws the “original” expression as a single waveform. The bottom half draws the expanded form as separate coloured components that sum to the same curve.

// Sum-sin mode
const original = Math.sin(omega * t + a);
const part1 = Math.sin(omega * t) * Math.cos(a);
const part2 = Math.cos(omega * t) * Math.sin(a);
// part1 + part2 === original

Both curves are overlaid so you can verify they match exactly. Switch modes to explore double-angle and power-reduction identities the same way.
explorations.overview: Trig identities are shown as wave transformations: a sum-to-product identity decomposes one wave into two component waves, a double-angle formula reshapes the waveform, and power reduction changes amplitude and offset. The overlay of original versus expanded forms makes each identity visually verifiable.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens when two waves collide?

explorations.title: Calculus of Trigonometry
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

In Derivative mode, the numerical derivative is computed using central differences:

const h = 0.001;
const dfdx = (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2 * h);

In Integral mode, left-endpoint Riemann rectangles accumulate the running sum:

let sum = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
  sum += f(x_i) * dx;
}

Increase the resolution slider to see the numerical result converge toward the exact analytical answer (shown as the dashed overlay).
explorations.overview: Derivatives and integrals of sine and cosine are computed numerically using central differences and Riemann sums, then overlaid with the exact analytical result. Watching the numerical approximation converge to the dashed analytical curve builds intuition for how differentiation and integration transform trigonometric functions.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What do you get when you differentiate a wave?

explorations.title: Heart Curve
explorations.tutorial:
Building the Heart

The y-coordinate is a sum of cosine harmonics with specific coefficients:

const coeffs = [13, -5, -2, -1];
let y = 0;
for (let n = 0; n < harmonics; n++) {
  y += coeffs[n] * Math.cos((n + 1) * t);
}
const x = 16 * Math.pow(Math.sin(t), 3);

Start with 1 harmonic (just 13·cos t) to see a lopsided ellipse, then add each successive term to watch the dip at the top deepen into the characteristic heart shape.
explorations.overview: A parametric heart curve is built from a small set of cosine harmonics whose coefficients are tuned to produce the iconic heart shape. Each harmonic adds one frequency component, illustrating Fourier synthesis in a visually memorable form. Toggle individual harmonics to see how they combine into the final outline.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What does love look like in polar coordinates?

explorations.title: Butterfly Curve
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

The curve is traced in polar coordinates and converted to Cartesian for drawing:

const r = Math.exp(Math.sin(theta))
        - 2 * Math.cos(4 * theta)
        + Math.pow(Math.sin((2 * theta - Math.PI) / 24), 5);
const x = r * Math.cos(theta);
const y = r * Math.sin(theta);

An animated dot traces the curve from θ = 0 to θ = 2π·n, where n is the number of rotations. The trail colour is mapped to the current angle so each wing section gets a distinct hue.
explorations.overview: Temple Fay\u2019s butterfly curve is a single polar equation that produces an intricate wing-like pattern. The formula combines exponentials, cosines, and a fifth-power cosine term to achieve its detailed shape. Angle-mapped coloring highlights the curve\u2019s self-similar looping structure.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can one equation draw a butterfly?

explorations.title: Phasor Diagrams
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

Two phasors rotate in the complex plane. Their tip-to-tail sum gives the resultant:

const z1x = amp1 * Math.cos(freq1 * t);
const z1y = amp1 * Math.sin(freq1 * t);
const z2x = amp2 * Math.cos(freq2 * t + phase);
const z2y = amp2 * Math.sin(freq2 * t + phase);
const totalX = z1x + z2x;
const totalY = z1y + z2y;

The upper half shows the phasor diagram (complex plane). The lower half shows Re(ztotal) as a time-domain waveform, revealing beats or standing-wave patterns depending on the frequency relationship.
explorations.overview: Phasor diagrams represent sinusoidal signals as rotating complex vectors. Two phasors are summed tip-to-tail to visualize interference; the resulting amplitude and phase depend on the relative frequency and phase offset. A synchronized time-domain waveform shows the real-valued signal each phasor represents.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why do engineers represent waves as spinning arrows?

explorations.title: Reaction-Diffusion
explorations.tutorial:
How Nature Paints Patterns

Alan Turing proposed in 1952 that biological patterns — spots on a leopard, stripes on a zebra, the branching of coral — arise from just two chemicals diffusing and reacting. No blueprint, no master plan: the pattern self-organizes from instability in the reaction-diffusion equations.

The balance between feed rate F and kill rate k determines what emerges. Tiny changes in these parameters produce qualitatively different worlds.

Experiments

  • Start with Mitosis and watch spots divide like cells.
  • Try Stripes then click in an empty area — labyrinthine fingers grow outward.
  • Click or drag on the canvas to inject new chemical seeds.
  • Fine-tune F and k manually to explore the phase boundary between order and chaos.
  • Increase Steps/Frame to fast-forward the simulation.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How do leopard spots and zebra stripes form themselves?

explorations.title: Wave Packet
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The upper panel shows the wave packet y(x, t) as a solid curve traveling to the right. The blue waveform is the full signal; the dashed lines show the ±envelope.

The lower panel shows the Fourier spectrum |Y(k′)| — a Gaussian centered on the carrier wave number k with width proportional to 1/σ.

Things to try

  • Increase σ — the packet gets wider in space and narrower in k-space (and vice versa).
  • Switch to Quadratic dispersion — watch the packet spread over time while crests slide through the envelope (vp ≠ vg).
  • Compare group velocity (envelope motion) and phase velocity (crest motion) by watching which direction the peaks move relative to the envelope center.
  • Lower k to see fewer oscillations under the envelope — fewer cycles mean a broader spectrum.
explorations.overview: A wave packet is a Gaussian envelope modulating a sinusoidal carrier, illustrating the distinction between group velocity (envelope speed) and phase velocity (carrier speed). Dispersion spreads the packet over time as different frequency components travel at different speeds, a core concept in quantum mechanics and signal processing.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens when you add infinitely many waves together?

explorations.title: Pascal's Triangle
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

Each small cell represents one binomial coefficient C(n, k). Rows run top to bottom (n = 0 at the top); columns run left to right within each row.

Color encodes the value mod p: dark cells are divisible by p, bright cells are nonzero residues.

Things to Try

  • Set Modulus = 2 and increase rows \u2014 watch the Sierpinski gasket emerge.
  • Try Modulus = 3, 5, 7 to see increasingly intricate fractal tilings.
  • Use the Highlight Diagonal selector to trace Fibonacci numbers or powers of 2 through the triangle.
  • Switch color schemes to see different visual emphasis on the residue classes.
explorations.overview: Pascal\u2019s triangle arranges the binomial coefficients C(n,k) in a triangular grid where each entry is the sum of the two above it. Coloring entries by their value mod p reveals striking fractal patterns \u2014 mod 2 produces the Sierpinski gasket, while higher primes uncover nested self-similar structures of their own.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How much structure is hiding in a triangle of numbers?

explorations.title: Central Limit Theorem
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left panel shows the base distribution \u2014 the shape of a single random variable X.

The right panel shows the density of SN = X1 + \u2026 + XN, computed by iterated convolution. When the Gaussian overlay is enabled, a matching normal curve is drawn for comparison.

Things to Try

  • Start with Uniform and increment N \u2014 watch the flat density become bell-shaped remarkably fast.
  • Try Exponential (skewed) \u2014 the skewness vanishes as N grows.
  • Try Bimodal (two humps) \u2014 the humps merge into one Gaussian.
  • Try Dice to see the discrete version: a histogram converging to a bell curve.
  • Press Animate to auto-increment N and watch convergence in real time.
explorations.overview: The Central Limit Theorem says that sums of independent random variables converge to a Gaussian distribution regardless of the original shape. This exploration visualizes the convergence as iterated convolution: start with any base distribution and watch its sum density approach the bell curve as you add more terms.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does everything in nature end up looking like a bell curve?

explorations.title: Power Laws
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left plot shows the power law density on ordinary linear axes \u2014 a steep drop that makes it hard to see the tail.

The right plot shows the same function on log-log axes. The power law becomes a straight line. Compare with exponential (orange) and Gaussian (green) overlays that curve away, revealing the fat tail.

Things to Try

  • Change \u03b1 \u2014 smaller \u03b1 means heavier tails (more extreme events).
  • Toggle Show Comparisons to see how exponential and Gaussian distributions differ on log-log axes.
  • Select a dataset to see synthetic data points that follow known power laws.
  • Increase x Range to see how the straight line extends indefinitely on log-log.
explorations.overview: Power-law distributions appear in phenomena from earthquake magnitudes to city sizes. On linear axes the heavy tail is barely visible; on log-log axes it becomes a straight line whose slope is the exponent \u03B1. Compare power laws to exponential and log-normal distributions to see why the tail matters.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why do cities, earthquakes, and words all follow the same law?

explorations.title: \u03B5-\u03B4 Definition of Limits
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

The horizontal cyan band shows the \u03B5-tolerance around the limit value L. The vertical yellow band shows the corresponding \u03B4-neighborhood around x = a.

// Find largest \u03B4 such that |f(x) - L| < \u03B5
// for all x in (a - \u03B4, a + \u03B4)
function findDelta(f, a, L, epsilon) {
  const step = 0.001;
  let delta = 0;
  for (let d = step; d < 5; d += step) {
    let ok = true;
    for (let x = a - d; x <= a + d; x += step) {
      if (Math.abs(f(x) - L) >= epsilon) {
        ok = false; break;
      }
    }
    if (ok) delta = d; else break;
  }
  return delta;
}

Try the piecewise function at x = 1 to see a discontinuity where \u03B4 = 0 for any \u03B5 < 2. Use the Animate \u03B5\u21920 button to watch the \u03B4-band shrink in tandem with \u03B5 for continuous functions.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How close is close enough to prove a limit exists?

explorations.title: The ε-δ Game
explorations.tutorial:
How to Play

Pick a function and a point a. Then:

  1. Set your ε challenge with the slider — make it as small as you dare.
  2. Press Challenge! — the computer searches for a valid δ.
  3. Watch the δ-band appear. The round is logged in the scoreboard.
  4. Press Auto-Play to let the computer auto-challenge itself with progressively tinier ε values.

Key insight

For continuous functions, the computer always wins: δ shrinks alongside ε, but never hits zero. For discontinuous functions (piecewise jump) or oscillating functions (sin(1/x) at 0), there is an ε threshold below which δ = 0 — the limit fails and the game is over.

Things to try

  • x² at any point — the computer always wins. Notice δ ≈ ε/(2|a|) for small ε.
  • sin(1/x) at x = 0 — the function oscillates infinitely fast and no δ works.
  • Piecewise at x = 1 — there's a jump of size 1, so any ε < 1 defeats the computer.
explorations.overview: An adversarial game that brings the \u03B5-\u03B4 definition of a limit to life. You set an error tolerance \u03B5 and the computer responds with a \u03B4-neighborhood. If f(x) stays within the \u03B5-band for every x in the \u03B4-band, the limit exists. Try functions with discontinuities or wild oscillations to stump the defender.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can the computer always find a response to your challenge?

explorations.title: Derivative Definition
explorations.tutorial:
From Slope to Speed

What does "instantaneous rate of change" mean? You can measure average speed between two points — that's the slope of the secant line. But what about speed at a single instant? The derivative answers this by taking the limit as the two points merge.

const slope = (f(x0 + h) - f(x0)) / h;
// As h \u2192 0, this slope approaches f'(x\u2080)

Experiments

  • Start with large h and watch the secant line cut roughly through the curve.
  • Shrink h toward zero — the secant pivots into the tangent. The slope readout converges to f'(x\u2080).
  • Press Animate h\u21920 to watch the transition happen smoothly.
  • Drag x\u2080 across the domain — green dots accumulate in the lower panel, tracing out the entire derivative function.
  • Switch to sin(x): the green dots should trace cos(x).
explorations.teaserQuestion: What does "instantaneous speed" actually mean?

explorations.title: Chain Rule Visualization
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

Three panels show the inner function f, the outer function g, and their composition g(f(x)). At each point a, tangent lines reveal the local slope:

const fPrime = (f(a + h) - f(a - h)) / (2 * h);
const gPrime = (g(f(a) + h) - g(f(a) - h)) / (2 * h);
const chainSlope = gPrime * fPrime;

Below the panels, stretching bars show how an infinitesimal interval dx is first stretched by f'(a) to produce du, then by g'(f(a)) to produce dy. The final bar length equals the chain rule product.

Drag a across the domain to see how the derivative of the composition varies. Notice that when either f' or g' is zero, the composition's derivative is also zero.
explorations.overview: The chain rule says the derivative of a composition g(f(x)) is g\u2019(f(x))\u00B7f\u2019(x). Three linked panels show f, g, and g\u2218f with tangent lines at the current point. Stretching bars visualize how a small change dx is first scaled by f\u2019 into du, then by g\u2019 into dy \u2014 making the multiplicative nature of the chain rule tangible.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why do rates of change multiply when functions compose?

explorations.title: Taylor Approximation Error
explorations.tutorial:
Exploring Taylor Error

This visualization frames the Taylor expansion as an error minimization problem rather than a series construction.

// Taylor polynomial centered at a
function taylor(x, a, N, derivs) {
  let sum = 0;
  for (let n = 0; n <= N; n++) {
    sum += derivs[n] * Math.pow(x - a, n) / factorial(n);
  }
  return sum;
}

// Integrated squared error
function error(a, R, N) {
  let E = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {
    const x = (a - R) + (2 * R * i / 1000);
    const diff = f(x) - taylor(x, a, N, derivs);
    E += diff * diff * (2 * R / 1000);
  }
  return E;
}

Things to Try

  • Move the center a — the error is smallest near a and grows away from it.
  • Increase N and watch the red shaded error region shrink.
  • Try ln(1+x) — the series diverges for x ≤ −1, so the error blows up on the left.
  • Compare ex (entire function, converges everywhere) with ln(1+x) (finite radius of convergence).
  • The right panel shows |f(x)−TN(x)|² and the total integrated error.
explorations.overview: A Taylor polynomial approximates a function near a center point by matching successive derivatives. This exploration frames the approximation as error minimization: watch the integrated squared error shrink as you add terms, and see how the region of good fit expands outward from the center.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How many terms does it take to approximate a function?

explorations.title: Manual Taylor Coefficient Fit
explorations.formulaShort: P_N(x) = c0 + c1x + c2x^2 + ... + cN x^N
explorations.formula: P_N(x) = c0 + c1x + c2x^2 + ... + cN x^N
explorations.tutorial:
How To Play

  • Pick a target: choose sin(x), cos(x), ex, or e−x2.
  • Set degree: higher degree gives more flexibility.
  • Tune coefficients: move c0, c1, ... sliders to reduce error.
  • Read score: lower MSE means better fit; score rises as fit improves.
explorations.overview: Instead of computing Taylor coefficients analytically, try tuning them by hand. Adjust each polynomial coefficient with a slider and watch the approximation improve or worsen. A running score shows how close your manual fit is to the optimal Taylor expansion, building intuition for what each coefficient controls.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you beat the built-in Taylor guess by hand?

explorations.title: Riemann Sums & Integration
explorations.tutorial:
Exploring Numerical Integration

// Left Riemann sum
function leftRiemann(f, a, b, n) {
  const dx = (b - a) / n;
  let sum = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++)
    sum += f(a + i * dx) * dx;
  return sum;
}
// Simpson's rule (n must be even)
function simpson(f, a, b, n) {
  const dx = (b - a) / n;
  let sum = f(a) + f(b);
  for (let i = 1; i < n; i++)
    sum += (i % 2 === 0 ? 2 : 4) * f(a + i * dx);
  return sum * dx / 3;
}

Things to Try

  • Increase n and watch rectangles shrink to fill the curve.
  • Compare Left vs Midpoint — midpoint converges much faster.
  • The log-log plot slopes reveal each method’s convergence order.
  • Simpson’s on x² gives the exact answer even for small n.
  • Press Animate to watch n increase and error shrink.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What if you measured area with infinitely many rectangles?

explorations.title: Random Walk
explorations.tutorial:
The Drunkard's Walk

Imagine a person taking steps in random directions. How far do they get after N steps? Not N steps away — only √N. This sub-linear scaling is the signature of diffusion, and it governs everything from molecules bouncing in a gas to stock prices wandering over time.

Run many walkers simultaneously and their endpoints form a Gaussian bell curve — a direct demonstration of the Central Limit Theorem.

Experiments

  • Run a single walker in 1D — the path zigzags unpredictably within the √N envelope.
  • Add more walkers to see the bell-curve histogram emerge on the right panel.
  • Increase the step count and watch the RMS grow as √N, not N.
  • Switch to 2D — the distance distribution follows a Rayleigh distribution, not a Gaussian.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How far does a random walker drift from where they started?

explorations.title: Law of Large Numbers
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The upper panel shows the running average X̄n for each independent trial as a colored line. The dashed white horizontal line marks E[X], the true expected value.

The lower panel shows a live histogram of all raw samples pooled from every trial. Its shape reveals the underlying distribution.

Things to Try

  • Start with Coin Flip — averages converge to 0.5.
  • Try Die Roll — averages converge to 3.5.
  • Try Exponential — averages converge to 1.0 despite the skewed distribution.
  • Now try Cauchy — the averages never settle down! Large outliers keep yanking the mean around, because E[X] doesn’t exist.
  • Increase Number of Trials to see many lines all funneling toward the mean (or wildly diverging for Cauchy).
  • Increase Max Samples to watch convergence over a longer horizon.
explorations.overview: The Law of Large Numbers guarantees that sample averages converge to the expected value as the number of trials grows. Multiple independent running averages are plotted simultaneously so you can see the convergence envelope tighten. Try the Cauchy distribution to see a case where convergence fails.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Do averages always stabilize?

explorations.title: Bayesian Updating
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The plot shows the probability density over θ ∈ [0, 1]. The dashed gray curve is the initial prior. The filled purple curve is the current posterior, which sharpens as more coin flips are observed.

The red vertical line marks the true θ used to generate the coin flips. The posterior should concentrate around this line as evidence grows.

Things to Try

  • Use the Uniform prior Beta(1,1) — it starts flat and lets the data speak.
  • Use a Strong prior Beta(10,10) centered at 0.5 — see how it resists early data but eventually yields.
  • Set True θ far from 0.5 (e.g., 0.2 or 0.8) with a strong prior — watch the tug-of-war between prior and data.
  • Watch the posterior mean α/(α+β) converge to the true θ.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How should you update your beliefs when new evidence arrives?

explorations.title: Markov Chain
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left panel shows the state graph. States are arranged in a polygon. Arrows between states have thickness proportional to transition probability. The glowing dot shows the current state of the chain.

The right panel shows a bar chart of visit frequencies alongside the theoretical stationary distribution (hollow rectangles).

Things to Try

  • Weather: 3-state chain (Sunny/Rainy/Cloudy). Frequencies converge to the stationary distribution within a few hundred steps.
  • PageRank: 4-page web graph. Watch which page gets the most visits.
  • Symmetric: Equal transition probabilities — stationary distribution is uniform.
  • Gambler: Absorbing states at 0 and 4. The walker eventually gets trapped.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Does a random process always forget where it came from?

explorations.title: Normal Distribution Explorer
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left panel shows the probability density function (bell curve). When the 68-95-99.7 rule is enabled, the sigma bands are shaded: 1σ (68%), 2σ (95%), 3σ (99.7%).

A semi-transparent histogram of random samples is overlaid to show how empirical data matches the theoretical curve.

The right panel shows the theoretical CDF (cyan sigmoid) alongside the empirical CDF (purple staircase) — the sorted samples plotted from 0 to 1. As the number of samples grows, the staircase converges to the smooth theoretical curve.

Things to Try

  • Move μ to shift the distribution left or right.
  • Increase σ to make it wider and shorter (but total area stays 1).
  • Increase Number of Samples to see the histogram converge to the curve.
  • Toggle the 68-95-99.7 rule to see the sigma bands.
explorations.overview: An interactive Gaussian explorer showing the probability density function with the 68-95-99.7 rule, the cumulative distribution function, and a live sample histogram. Adjust the mean \u03BC and standard deviation \u03C3 to see how the bell curve shifts and spreads, and draw samples to watch the histogram converge to the PDF.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What makes the bell curve so universal?

explorations.title: 1D Wave Equation
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The upper panel shows the string displacement u(x, t) as a cyan curve. The x-axis represents the string at rest. Displacement is shown both above and below this equilibrium.

The lower panel is a space-time (waterfall) diagram. Each vertical slice is a snapshot of the string at one moment. Time flows left to right. Colors use a diverging map: red = positive, blue = negative, white = zero.

Things to try

  • Start with Gaussian pulse + Fixed boundaries. Watch the pulse split, bounce off the walls (inverting each time), and eventually form standing waves.
  • Switch to Free boundaries \u2014 reflections preserve sign. Compare the waterfall patterns.
  • Try Absorbing boundaries \u2014 the pulse leaves cleanly, no reflections.
  • Increase damping to see exponential decay of the wave energy.
  • The Standing wave initial condition sets up a perfect mode shape \u2014 with fixed boundaries it oscillates in place forever (zero damping).
  • Increase wave speed c to see faster propagation in the waterfall diagram.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What law governs every ripple, every sound, every vibration?

explorations.title: K\u00E1rm\u00E1n Vortex Street
explorations.tutorial:
How This Simulation Works

A uniform flow enters from the left and encounters a circular cylinder. At low Reynolds numbers (high viscosity or slow flow), the wake is steady. As Re increases, the wake becomes unstable and vortices begin shedding alternately from the top and bottom of the cylinder.

Visualization Modes

  • Dye: Colored dye streaks injected at the left boundary reveal the flow structure. Watch for the alternating pattern downstream of the cylinder.
  • Velocity: Shows the velocity magnitude overlaid on the dye. Bright areas indicate fast flow.
  • Vorticity: Shows the curl of the velocity field. Red = counter-clockwise, blue = clockwise rotation. The alternating red-blue pattern downstream is the K\u00E1rm\u00E1n street.

Things to try

  • Start with default settings and watch the vortex street develop.
  • Increase viscosity \u2014 the wake stabilizes (lower Re). The vortices stop shedding.
  • Increase inflow speed \u2014 more vigorous shedding (higher Re).
  • Move the cylinder off-center (change cylinderY) to break the symmetry and see how it affects shedding.
  • Switch to vorticity view to see the clean alternating vortex pattern.
explorations.overview: Flow past a cylinder produces periodic vortex shedding known as the K\u00E1rm\u00E1n vortex street. This lattice-Boltzmann simulation shows how symmetric flow breaks into an alternating pattern of vortices. Switch between dye, velocity, and vorticity visualizations to see the structure from different perspectives.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why do flags flutter and bridges sway in the wind?

explorations.title: Van der Pol Oscillator
explorations.tutorial:
Things to Try

  • Small μ (≈ 0.1): the limit cycle is nearly sinusoidal — compare visually with a pure circle in the phase plane.
  • Large μ (≥ 4): the waveform becomes a relaxation oscillation — long slow segments punctuated by fast jumps.
  • Watch transients: start far from the cycle and see how quickly orbits spiral inward or outward.
  • Time waveform: compare the right-panel x(t) trace at μ=0.1 vs μ=5 — how does the period change?
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens when a circuit fights its own damping?

explorations.title: RLC Filter
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

The top panel shows the Bode magnitude plot — gain in dB vs. log frequency. The bottom panel shows the time-domain input and output waveforms at the current drive frequency.

Things to Try

  • Increase R to see the resonance peak flatten (more damping, lower Q).
  • Watch the resonance peak in the Bode plot — the dashed line marks ω₀ = 1/√(LC).
  • Switch between Low-pass / High-pass / Band-pass to compare filter shapes.
  • Drag the drive frequency across the resonance and watch the output waveform change amplitude and phase.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does a capacitor decide which frequencies to let through?

explorations.title: Diode Clipper
explorations.formulaShort: y = f(x) where f is a nonlinear transfer curve
explorations.formula: y = f(x) where f is a nonlinear transfer curve
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Increase Drive to push the sine harder into the transfer curve — watch the FFT fill with odd harmonics (symmetric curves) or all harmonics (asymmetric).
  • Switch between Curve types to see how the transfer shape controls harmonic content.
  • Add DC Bias to break symmetry and reveal even harmonics in otherwise odd-only curves.
  • Change Input Frequency to shift where harmonics land in the spectrum.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Where do all those extra harmonics come from?

explorations.title: Chua's Circuit
explorations.tutorial:
How To Explore

  • Double scroll: watch the classic two-lobed attractor form at α ≈ 15.6.
  • Single scroll: lower α to ~12 and see the trajectory collapse to one lobe.
  • Periodic orbit: at α ≈ 8.5 the chaos gives way to a stable limit cycle.
  • Tune α: sweep it slowly to observe bifurcations between regimes.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can three components and a battery create chaos?

explorations.title: Phase-Locked Loop
explorations.tutorial:
How To Use This Demo

  • Watch phase lock: with small frequency offset the phase error converges to zero.
  • Cycle slipping: increase the frequency offset until the loop can no longer lock — the phase winds continuously.
  • Damping & bandwidth: tune ζ and ωn to see underdamped ringing vs. overdamped sluggishness.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does your radio stay tuned to exactly the right frequency?

explorations.title: Relaxation Oscillator
explorations.tutorial:
How To Use This Demo

  • RC Time Constant: decrease to speed up charging/discharging, increase to slow it down.
  • Thresholds: narrow the hysteresis band (Vhigh − Vlow) to see faster switching.
  • Hysteresis Loop: the right panel traces Vc vs Output — watch the rectangular path and directional arrows.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How do you build a clock from a capacitor and a switch?

explorations.title: Bucket Brigade Delay
explorations.tutorial:
How To Use This Demo

  • Watch the signal ripple down the bucket chain from left to right.
  • Stages: increase the number of stages to lengthen the delay.
  • Clock Rate: controls how many shift-ticks occur per second.
  • Clock Modulation: enable to sweep the clock rate and hear a chorus effect in the waveform panel.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What if you could literally pass a voltage from hand to hand?

explorations.title: Colpitts Oscillator
explorations.tutorial:
Things to Try

  • Watch startup: the oscillation builds from tiny noise — watch the waveform panel on the left as amplitude grows to steady state.
  • Shift frequency: change the C₁/C₂ ratio or L to move the oscillation frequency — check the spectrum panel on the right.
  • Kill oscillation: reduce Feedback Gain below the threshold (~damping) and the oscillation dies out.
  • Damping vs gain: increase Loss — you'll need more gain to sustain oscillation.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does a circuit decide what note to sing?

explorations.title: Memristor Chaos
explorations.tutorial:
How To Explore

  • Compare with Chua's circuit: the memristor replaces the piecewise-linear diode with a smooth nonlinearity, yielding richer dynamics.
  • Vary memristor params: sweep a and b to find coexisting attractors — some initial conditions converge to one wing, others to the opposite wing.
  • Try different initial conditions: reset and tweak starting values to reveal hidden attractors that share the same parameter set.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What if a resistor could remember how much current flowed through it?

explorations.title: Ring Oscillator
explorations.tutorial:
Things to Try

  • Add more stages and watch the oscillation frequency drop.
  • Change gate delay τ to speed up or slow down the ring.
  • Odd stages oscillate while even stages latch to a fixed state — try it!
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you make something oscillate just by saying 'not' enough times?

explorations.title: Charge Pump
explorations.tutorial:
How To Explore

  • Watch the staircase build up cycle by cycle as each capacitor charges.
  • Add more stages for higher voltage — but more stages mean slower convergence.
  • Increase Load Current to see voltage droop under load.
  • Larger Cap Size means faster convergence and less ripple.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can capacitors multiply voltage out of thin air?

explorations.title: Modular Multiplication Circle
explorations.tutorial:
How The Visualization Works

N points are placed at equal angles on a circle. For each point i, a line is drawn to point (i × k) mod N.

Things to Try

  • Set k = 2 to see the classic cardioid (1 cusp).
  • Set k = 3 for a nephroid (2 cusps), k = 4 for 3 cusps, etc.
  • Slowly increase k — the curves morph continuously through beautiful intermediary shapes.
  • Increase N to 200+ for smoother envelope curves.
  • Try non-integer multipliers for surprising patterns.
explorations.overview: Place N points on a circle and draw a line from each point i to (i\u00D7k) mod N. Depending on the multiplier k, cardioids, nephroids, and epicycloids emerge from pure modular arithmetic. Sweeping k continuously reveals how these families of curves transform into one another.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does a simple multiplication table draw a perfect cardioid?

explorations.title: Ulam Spiral
explorations.formulaShort: n ↦ spiral position; highlight if prime
explorations.formula: n ↦ spiral position; highlight if prime
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

Each cell represents a natural number, arranged in a counter-clockwise spiral from the center. Bright cells are primes; dark cells are composite.

Things to Try

  • Increase the grid size to see more of the spiral — diagonals become clearer at larger scales.
  • Switch to Twin Primes mode to see pairs (p, p+2) highlighted.
  • Enable Quadratic Overlay to trace n² + n + 41 along the spiral.
  • Try different color schemes to emphasize different patterns.
explorations.overview: The Ulam spiral arranges natural numbers in a spiral and highlights the primes. Mysterious diagonal lines appear, revealing that certain quadratic polynomials are unusually rich in primes. Toggle twin-prime highlighting and quadratic overlays to explore the deep structure hidden in prime distribution.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why do primes line up in diagonals when you spiral the integers?

explorations.title: Ulam Sphere
explorations.formulaShort: Shell k: 6k points via Fibonacci sphere; highlight primes
explorations.formula: Shell k: 6k points via Fibonacci sphere; highlight primes
explorations.tutorial:
Exploring the Sphere

Each point on the sphere represents a natural number. Bright glowing points are primes; composites are hidden by default.

Things to Try

  • Drag to rotate the sphere and look for clusters or voids.
  • Increase Shells to pack more integers (40 shells ≈ 5000 integers, 80 shells ≈ 19000).
  • Switch to Twin Primes to see pairs (p, p±2) — do they cluster on certain shells?
  • Enable Quadratic Overlay to highlight primes from n²+n+41 and 4n²+1.
  • Toggle Show Composites to see the full integer lattice.
  • Try different color schemes to reveal depth and shell structure.
explorations.overview:
The 2D Ulam spiral maps integers onto a square spiral and highlights primes,
revealing diagonal patterns from prime-rich quadratic polynomials. This exploration extends the
concept to 3D: integers are distributed across concentric spherical shells using a Fibonacci
lattice, with primes rendered as glowing points. The goal is to discover whether new structural
patterns emerge when the integer-to-geometry mapping gains an extra dimension.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Do prime patterns survive the jump from 2D spirals to 3D spheres?

explorations.title: Ulam Helix
explorations.formulaShort: (x, y) = spiral(n), z = n · zScale; highlight if prime
explorations.formula: (x, y) = spiral(n), z = n · zScale; highlight if prime
explorations.tutorial:
Exploring the Helix

Each point represents a natural number placed on a square spiral, then lifted by its value. Bright glowing points are primes; composites are hidden by default.

Things to Try

  • Drag to rotate and view the helix from different angles.
  • Set Z Scale = 0 to see the flat Ulam spiral in 3D.
  • Increase Z Scale to stretch the helix and reveal vertical prime gaps.
  • Switch to Twin Primes to see pairs (p, p±2) highlighted.
  • Enable Quadratic Overlay to trace primes from n²+n+41 and 4n²+1.
  • Toggle Show Composites to see the full integer lattice.
explorations.overview:
The 2D Ulam spiral maps integers onto a square spiral and highlights primes,
revealing diagonal patterns from prime-rich quadratic polynomials. This exploration extends the
concept by adding a z-coordinate proportional to the integer value, turning the flat spiral into
a helix. The side view reveals vertical clustering patterns in the prime distribution that are
invisible in the traditional 2D view.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What patterns emerge when you stand the Ulam spiral up into a helix?

explorations.title: Ford Circles
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The x-axis represents the number line from 0 to 1. Each circle sits tangent to the axis at a rational point p/q, with size inversely proportional to q².

Things to Try

  • Increase Max Denominator to reveal finer structure between existing circles.
  • Enable Show Labels to see the fraction at each circle.
  • Highlight a specific Farey Level to see which fractions appear at each stage of the Farey sequence.
  • Notice how every new circle fits perfectly in a gap — tangent to its neighbors, never overlapping.
explorations.overview: Ford circles assign each reduced fraction p/q a circle tangent to the x-axis with radius 1/(2q\u00B2). Remarkably, these circles never overlap \u2014 they are tangent precisely when the corresponding fractions are Farey neighbors. Increase the maximum denominator to watch the packing fill in and reveal the mediant structure of the rationals.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How can you pack a circle for every fraction without any overlaps?

explorations.title: Euclidean Algorithm Rectangles
explorations.formulaShort: gcd(a, b) via repeated square subtraction
explorations.formula: gcd(a, b) via repeated square subtraction
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The large rectangle has dimensions a × b. Colored squares show successive cuts of the Euclidean algorithm. The readout shows the continued fraction expansion and the GCD.

Things to Try

  • Set a = 89, b = 55 (consecutive Fibonacci numbers) to see the golden-ratio spiral.
  • Try a = 100, b = 1 to see a single long sequence of unit squares.
  • Try a = 144, b = 89 for a deeper Fibonacci subdivision.
  • Watch how the continued fraction coefficients correspond directly to the number of squares at each step.
explorations.overview: The Euclidean algorithm finds the GCD of two numbers by repeated subtraction. Here it is visualized as geometry: cut the largest possible square from a rectangle, then repeat on the remainder. The colored squares correspond to the quotients in the continued-fraction expansion, connecting number theory to geometric proportion.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What does the greatest common divisor look like as a picture?

explorations.title: Gaussian Primes
explorations.formulaShort: N(a + bi) = a² + b²; prime if N is prime form
explorations.formula: N(a + bi) = a² + b²; prime if N is prime form
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

Each dot represents a Gaussian integer a + bi. Bright dots are Gaussian primes; dark dots are composite or zero.

Things to Try

  • Look along the real axis: primes ≡ 3 (mod 4) like 3, 7, 11 appear as dots, but 5, 13 do not (they split).
  • Find the split primes: 5 = (2+i)(2−i), so dots appear at 2+i and 2−i.
  • Switch Color Mode to "By Type" to visually separate splits, inerts, and ramified.
  • Increase the range to see the four-fold symmetry of the pattern.
explorations.overview: Gaussian primes are the prime elements of the Gaussian integers \u2124[i]. Ordinary primes that are 3 mod 4 stay prime in \u2124[i], while primes that are 1 mod 4 split into conjugate pairs. The resulting pattern in the complex plane reveals a fourfold symmetry and unexpected visual structure in prime factorization.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Which primes can be written as the sum of two squares?

explorations.title: Surprise & Entropy
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

The left panel shows probability bars you can drag up/down and surprise markers (diamonds) for each outcome. The right panel shows the entropy gauge and how it compares to the maximum log2 N.

Things to Try

  • Start with 2 outcomes (a coin). Make it fair, then biased — watch entropy drop.
  • Increase to 6 outcomes. Set all equal for max entropy, then concentrate mass on one.
  • Try to create the lowest-entropy non-degenerate distribution you can.
  • Notice: high-probability outcomes have low surprise; low-probability outcomes have high surprise.
explorations.overview: Shannon entropy measures the average surprise of a probability distribution. Drag the probability bars to reshape the distribution and watch the entropy gauge respond: a uniform distribution maximizes entropy while a peaked one minimizes it. Surprise markers on each bar show how rare events carry more information than common ones.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Which distribution is the most surprising on average?

explorations.title: Source Coding (Huffman)
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left panel shows the Huffman binary tree. Each leaf is a symbol; internal nodes show merged frequency. The path from root to leaf gives the codeword (left = 0, right = 1).

The right panel shows a table of symbols with their frequencies, codewords, and code lengths, plus aggregate statistics.

Things to Try

  • Use English Letters and notice common letters get short codes.
  • Switch to Uniform (4) — all codes are 2 bits, exactly log2 4.
  • Try Highly Skewed — the dominant symbol gets a 1-bit code.
  • Compare L to H: how close does Huffman get?
explorations.overview: Huffman coding assigns short bit strings to frequent symbols and long ones to rare symbols. This exploration builds the tree live from symbol frequencies and shows how the average code length approaches Shannon\u2019s entropy bound \u2014 the theoretical minimum for lossless compression. Skew the distribution to see the tree become highly asymmetric.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you compress a message below its entropy?

explorations.title: Noisy Channel
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left panel shows the channel diagram with animated bit streams and crossover arrows. The right panel shows the entropy Venn diagram and the capacity curve.

Things to Try

  • Set ε = 0 (noiseless) — capacity = 1 bit, I = H(X).
  • Set ε = 0.5 (pure noise) — capacity = 0, output is independent of input.
  • Fix ε and vary input bias p — watch I(X;Y) peak at p = 0.5.
  • Trace the capacity curve: C = 1 − H(ε).
explorations.overview: A binary symmetric channel flips each bit with probability \u03B5.
