This exploration visualizes mutual information as the overlap region in an entropy Venn diagram and plots channel capacity C = 1 \u2212 H(\u03B5) as a function of the error rate. At \u03B5 = 0.5 the channel destroys all information; at \u03B5 = 0 it is perfect.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How much information can you push through a noisy wire?

explorations.title: KL Divergence
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The top panels show P (blue) and Q (purple) as bar charts, side by side. The bottom-left shows DKL(P‖Q) with per-bin contributions. The bottom-right shows DKL(Q‖P). Comparing the two makes the asymmetry vivid.

Things to Try

  • Start with Nearly Identical — both divergences are small and similar.
  • Switch to Sparse vs Spread — one direction is much larger.
  • Try Shifted Peak — P and Q are mirror images but divergences differ if shapes differ.
  • Use Swap P↔Q to feel the asymmetry.
explorations.overview: Kullback\u2013Leibler divergence D_KL(P\u2016Q) measures how much distribution P diverges from a reference Q, in units of extra bits. Crucially, it is asymmetric: D_KL(P\u2016Q) \u2260 D_KL(Q\u2016P). Side-by-side bar charts and divergence gauges make this asymmetry visually obvious. Swap P and Q to see the difference directly.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does KL divergence care which distribution is "true"?

explorations.title: Shannon–Boltzmann Bridge
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left panel shows N colored particles in M compartments. Particles attempt random hops each tick. The top-right panel shows the occupation histogram. The bottom-right shows both entropies over time, normalized to [0, 1], along with the Stirling bridge identity.

Things to Try

  • Start with all particles in one box and watch equilibration.
  • Increase N to 200 — the two entropy curves converge more tightly.
  • Switch to a linear or well energy landscape. The dashed yellow line on the histogram shows the predicted Boltzmann distribution — watch the bars converge to it.
  • With a non-flat landscape, drag Temperature from high to low. Particles concentrate in low-energy compartments; entropy drops.
  • Set T very high (> 15) — the landscape barely matters; you recover the flat (uniform) limit. This is why “infinite temperature” means maximum disorder.
  • Use 16–20 compartments with a well landscape — the Boltzmann peak at the center is unmistakable.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does the same formula measure both information and thermodynamic disorder — and what role does temperature play?

explorations.title: Least Action Paths
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

The main panel shows the true parabolic trajectory (gold) and two alternative paths (cyan, pink) with draggable control points. The right panel shows the action S computed for each path.

Things to Try

  • Drag the cyan/pink control points up or down and watch their action increase.
  • Try to make an alternative path with lower action than the parabola (you can’t!).
  • Change the throw angle and note the parabola reshapes but always minimizes S.
explorations.overview: The principle of least action says that the true trajectory of a projectile is the one that minimizes the action integral S = \u222B(T \u2212 V) dt. Drag alternative paths and compare their action to the true parabola \u2014 every deviation increases S, making the physical path a genuine minimum.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does nature choose the parabola over every other path?

explorations.title: Brachistochrone
explorations.formulaShort: x = R(θ − sinθ), y = R(1 − cosθ)
explorations.formula: x = R(θ − sinθ), y = R(1 − cosθ)
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

Four beads race from A (top-left) to B (bottom-right) along different curves. Press Start to race them. The elapsed time for each is shown in the right panel.

Things to Try

  • Run the race at default settings — the cycloid always wins.
  • Move B closer horizontally — the cycloid dips steeper.
  • Note the straight line is simple but slow; the parabola is better but still loses.
explorations.overview: The brachistochrone problem asks: what curve gives the fastest slide between two points under gravity? Four beads race simultaneously down a straight line, parabola, circular arc, and the optimal cycloid. The cycloid always wins \u2014 a result first proved by Johann Bernoulli using the calculus of variations.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Which ramp shape gets a ball from A to B fastest?

explorations.title: Fermat's Principle
explorations.formulaShort: sin θ₁ / sin θ₂ = v₁ / v₂
explorations.formula: sin θ₁ / sin θ₂ = v₁ / v₂
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The left panel shows light traveling from source A in medium 1 (top, speed v1) to point B in medium 2 (bottom, speed v2). Drag the refraction point along the interface. The right panel shows the travel time as a function of interface position, with the minimum marked.

Things to Try

  • Drag the refraction point — notice time increases away from the optimum.
  • Set v2 much slower than v1 (like air → glass) — the bend is dramatic.
  • Set v1 = v2 — light goes straight (no refraction).
  • Compare the measured angles to Snell’s law prediction.
explorations.overview: Fermat\u2019s principle states that light follows the path of least travel time. Drag the refraction point along the interface between two media and watch Snell\u2019s law emerge: the refraction angle that minimizes total time exactly matches the ratio of wave speeds predicted by n\u2081 sin \u03B8\u2081 = n\u2082 sin \u03B8\u2082.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does light bend when it enters water?

explorations.title: Laplacian Growth
explorations.tutorial:
Reading the Visualization

The cluster grows from a seed (center, top edge, or ring) outward. Color encodes growth time: dark = early, bright = recent. The potential field is shown as a faint background gradient.

Things to Try

  • η = 1: wispy, diffusion-limited branches.
  • η = 2–3: sharper, more lightning-like.
  • η > 4: dense root/fracture networks with thick trunks.
  • Try Line seed for top-down lightning bolts, Point seed for radial growth.
  • Larger grids give finer detail but grow slower.
explorations.overview: Laplacian growth models branching patterns like lightning, mineral dendrites, and root networks via the dielectric breakdown model. Particles aggregate at the cluster boundary with probability proportional to the local electric field raised to a power \u03B7, producing fractal structures whose branching density is tunable.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why do lightning bolts, river deltas, and cracked mud all branch the same way?

explorations.title: Gradient Dimensions
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Dimension n: Increase from 2 to 50. Each bar is one partial derivative.
  • Function: Parabola gives gradient 2xᵢ; cosine gives -sin(xᵢ).
  • Key idea: In infinite dimensions, the "coordinate" is a point (e.g. time t). The functional derivative δJ/δf(t) is the gradient component at that point.
explorations.overview: This exploration shows how a gradient vector grows with the number of dimensions. In 2-D the gradient has two components; in 50-D it has fifty. Each bar represents one partial derivative, building intuition for the leap to infinite-dimensional functional derivatives where every point on a curve contributes a "direction."
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens to the gradient as the number of dimensions grows?

explorations.title: Discrete Path Action
explorations.formulaShort: S = Σ L(tᵢ, yᵢ, vᵢ) Δt  →  ∫ L dt
explorations.formula: S = Σ L(tᵢ, yᵢ, vᵢ) Δt  →  ∫ L dt
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Control points N: Few points = polygonal path. Many = smooth. The path dimension is N.
  • Animate N: Sweep from 5 to 200 — watch the path smooth out and S converge.
  • Drag control points to reshape the alternative path. Its action always exceeds the true path.
explorations.overview: A discretized path with N control points lives in an N-dimensional space. The action S is an ordinary function of that vector, and as N increases the discrete sum converges to the continuous action integral. Drag points to see how any deviation from the true path raises the action.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens to the path as you add more control points?

explorations.title: Functional Derivative
explorations.formulaShort: δS/δy(t) — one derivative per time point
explorations.formula: δS/δy(t) — one derivative per time point
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Right panel: The curve shows ∂S/∂yᵢ at each control point. Zero = optimal (Euler-Lagrange satisfied).
  • True path: Gradient is zero — the parabola is optimal.
  • Drag control points to perturb the path. The gradient becomes non-zero where you perturbed.
explorations.overview: The functional derivative \u03B4S/\u03B4y(t) generalizes the partial derivative to function space: it tells you how the action changes when the path is nudged at a single point t. Where the gradient vanishes the path is optimal, recovering the Euler\u2013Lagrange equation in the continuous limit.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What is the gradient of the action with respect to the path?

explorations.title: Euler-Lagrange Bridge
explorations.tutorial:
The Bridge

Discrete: $\\nabla S = 0$ means $\\partial S/\\partial y_i = 0$ for each control point $i$.

Continuous: $\\delta S/\\delta y(t) = 0$ for all $t$ $\\Rightarrow$ Euler-Lagrange.

The parabola satisfies Euler-Lagrange. The gold curve is the solution.
explorations.overview: This exploration connects the finite-dimensional optimality condition \u2207S = 0 to the infinite-dimensional Euler\u2013Lagrange equation \u03B4S/\u03B4y = 0. The discrete gradient has one component per control point; in the continuum limit these become one equation per instant t \u2014 a differential equation that the true path must satisfy.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does "set the gradient to zero" become a differential equation?

explorations.title: Embedding Dimension
explorations.formulaShort: f ≈ Σₖ cₖ φₖ — truncate to n terms
explorations.formula: f ≈ Σₖ cₖ φₖ — truncate to n terms
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Embedding dimension n: Number of Fourier modes. Few = coarse; many = fine.
  • Target: The "true" function we're approximating. Bump needs many modes (sharp features).
  • Reconstruction error shrinks as n grows — but some targets need more dimensions than others.
explorations.overview: High-dimensional embeddings approximate objects that may live in an infinite-dimensional space. Here you choose a target function and reconstruct it with n Fourier modes \u2014 each mode adds one dimension to the representation. Reconstruction error shrinks as n grows, but sharp features need many more dimensions than smooth ones.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What are we approximating when we use 768-dimensional word embeddings?

explorations.title: ODE Integrator Playground
explorations.formulaShort: dy/dt = f(t,y) — Euler vs Midpoint vs RK4 vs RK5 (Fehlberg)
explorations.formula: dy/dt = f(t,y) — Euler vs Midpoint vs RK4 vs RK5 (Fehlberg)
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • System: Pick an ODE system — some have exact solutions for comparison.
  • Step size h: Increase to see methods diverge. Euler fails first.
  • Step mode: Toggle to advance one step at a time and watch error grow.
  • Stiff system: Try "Stiff System" with large h — explicit methods blow up.
  • RK5 (Fehlberg): Uses the 5th-order weights from the RKF45 tableau at fixed step size. The full adaptive RKF45 algorithm would also compute a 4th-order estimate to adjust h automatically — here all methods share the same h for a fair comparison.
explorations.overview:
This exploration is a side-by-side lab for comparing four classical ODE integration methods. Each method approximates the same ODE with the same step size, but they accumulate error at vastly different rates.

When an exact solution exists, a dashed gray reference line is shown and the error panel tracks cumulative deviation. For chaotic systems like Lorenz, there is no reference — watch how quickly the trajectories separate from each other.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How many steps before Euler diverges from the true solution?

explorations.title: Phase Portrait Builder
explorations.tutorial:
Seeing All Futures at Once

A phase portrait shows every possible trajectory of a 2D dynamical system in a single picture. Instead of asking "what happens from this initial condition?" you see the answer for all initial conditions simultaneously.

Nullclines (green and orange curves) are where one component of velocity is zero. Fixed points lie at their intersections — equilibria where the system stays put. The type of fixed point (stable, unstable, saddle, spiral) determines the local flow pattern.

Experiments

  • Click on the canvas to launch trajectories from any initial condition.
  • Try each preset and identify the fixed point types: ● stable, ○ unstable, ◇ saddle, ◎ spiral.
  • The Van der Pol preset has a limit cycle — all trajectories converge to the same closed loop.
  • Type your own equations to create custom vector fields.
explorations.overview: This is the Swiss army knife for rebuilding ODE intuition. Every 2D autonomous system can be understood through its phase portrait: the interplay of nullclines, fixed points, and the trajectories connecting them.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can you predict the trajectory from a given start just by looking at the vector field?

explorations.title: Stability Region Atlas
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Colored regions: Each color shows where |R(z)| ≤ 1 for that method.
  • Place eigenvalues: Click to drop a λh marker — if it's inside the region, the method is stable.
  • Step size h: Multiplies the eigenvalue positions to show which methods are stable at that step size.
  • Stiff test: Set a large negative eigenvalue and crank up h — explicit methods lose stability first.
explorations.overview: This visualization makes "stiffness" viscerally concrete. The stability region of a numerical method determines which step sizes won't blow up for a given ODE. Explicit methods (Euler, Midpoint, RK4) have finite stability regions; implicit methods cover the entire left half-plane, explaining why they handle stiff problems.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why do some ODEs require tiny step sizes even when the solution changes slowly?

explorations.title: Opinion Dynamics
explorations.formulaShort: x_i ← x_i + μ·Σ(x_j - x_i) if |x_j - x_i| < ε
explorations.formula: x_i ← x_i + μ·Σ(x_j - x_i) if |x_j - x_i| < ε
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Confidence ε: The main control. Below ~0.25, consensus breaks. Below ~0.15, many clusters form.
  • Network topology: Scale-free networks (with hubs) resist fragmentation; grids polarize locally.
  • Watch the histogram: It shows the opinion distribution evolving in real time.
  • Convergence rate μ: Faster convergence → sharper transitions.
explorations.overview: This simulation implements the Deffuant bounded confidence model on a network. The core mechanism is simple: connected agents adjust their opinions toward each other, but only if they already agree within a threshold ε. This creates echo chambers — groups that reinforce internally while drifting apart from outsiders.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How narrow must the confidence threshold be before a population splinters?

explorations.title: Graph Laplacian Spectral Modes
explorations.formulaShort: L = D - A, eigenmodes of heat diffusion on graphs
explorations.formula: L = D - A, eigenmodes of heat diffusion on graphs
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Mode selector: Step through eigenvectors 0, 1, 2, ... to see the vibration modes.
  • Mode 0: Constant everywhere (λ₀=0) — the graph is connected.
  • Mode 1 (Fiedler): Positive/negative halves reveal the graph's natural bisection.
  • Diffuse heat: Click a node to place heat, watch it decompose into decaying modes.
  • Barbell graph: Two cliques connected by one edge — dramatic spectral gap.
explorations.overview: The graph Laplacian's eigenvectors are the "resonant frequencies" of a network. Just as a vibrating string has fundamental and harmonic modes, a graph has spectral modes that govern how signals spread. This is the mathematical basis of spectral clustering, graph signal processing, and diffusion-based community detection.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How does a network reveal its communities through vibration?

explorations.title: Kuramoto Oscillators on Networks
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Coupling K: Increase to drive synchronization. Watch R climb from ~0 to ~1.
  • Frequency spread: Wider = harder to sync. Narrow + strong K = fast locking.
  • Two-clusters topology: Can one cluster sync independently of the other?
  • Ring: Look for chimera states — groups of locked nodes next to drifting ones.
explorations.overview: The Kuramoto model is the canonical model of synchronization on networks. Each node is an oscillator with its own natural frequency; coupling through graph edges pulls nearby phases together. The transition from incoherence to synchrony is a phase transition that depends on coupling strength, frequency distribution, and network topology.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Can half a network synchronize while the other half drifts?

explorations.title: Network Epidemic Simulator
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Infection rate β: Probability of transmission per contact per step.
  • Recovery rate γ: Probability of recovering each step.
  • Vaccination: Pre-immunize a fraction of the population at random.
  • Scale-free network: Hubs create superspreader dynamics — compare to grid.
  • Click a node to manually infect it and seed an outbreak.
explorations.overview: This simulation runs discrete-time SIR dynamics on a network. Unlike the mean-field ODE (which assumes homogeneous mixing), network structure creates dramatically different outcomes: scale-free networks are vulnerable to hubs, small-world networks allow rapid global spread, and grids produce wave-like fronts.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How many people must be vaccinated to prevent an epidemic on a scale-free network?

explorations.title: Bifurcation Anatomy
explorations.formulaShort: dx/dt = f(x, μ) — saddle-node, transcritical, pitchfork, Hopf
explorations.formula: dx/dt = f(x, μ) — saddle-node, transcritical, pitchfork, Hopf
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Choose a type: Each has its own normal form ODE.
  • Sweep μ: The parameter slider crosses the critical value. Watch fixed points move and change.
  • Phase portrait: The left panel shows the vector field and equilibria at current μ.
  • Bifurcation diagram: The right panel shows fixed point positions vs μ. Solid = stable, dashed = unstable.
explorations.overview: Every bifurcation you've seen in logistic maps and coupled systems reduces to one of these four normal forms near the critical parameter value. Understanding these archetypes gives you a vocabulary for reading ANY dynamical system's behavior as parameters change.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What happens to an equilibrium when it collides with another?

explorations.title: Delay Differential Equations
explorations.formulaShort: dx/dt = f(x(t), x(t-τ)) — Mackey-Glass, delayed logistic
explorations.formula: dx/dt = f(x(t), x(t-τ)) — Mackey-Glass, delayed logistic
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Delay τ: The main chaos knob. Increase it and watch the route to chaos.
  • Time series: Top panel shows x(t) — look for period doubling.
  • Phase plot: Bottom panel plots x(t) vs x(t−τ) — the "attractor" lives here.
  • Mackey-Glass: τ < 13 stable, τ ≈ 17 periodic, τ > 20 chaotic.
explorations.overview: Delay differential equations model systems with memory: the rate of change depends not just on the current state but on the state some time τ ago. This arises naturally in feedback systems (circuits, biology, network latency). The delay creates an infinite-dimensional phase space, enabling complex dynamics from deceptively simple equations.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How can a single-variable ODE produce chaos just by adding a delay?

explorations.title: Stochastic Resonance
explorations.tutorial:
How to Explore

  • Noise σ: The star control. Sweep from 0 to 2 and watch the SNR curve peak.
  • Forcing amplitude A: Subthreshold — too weak to push the particle over alone.
  • Watch the particle: At resonance, hops synchronize with the blue cosine wave.
  • SNR panel: Signal-to-noise ratio vs noise intensity — the classic resonance peak.
explorations.overview:
Stochastic resonance is one of the most counterintuitive phenomena in physics: adding noise to a weak signal makes the signal easier to detect. The mechanism requires three ingredients: a threshold or barrier, a subthreshold periodic signal, and noise. When the noise level matches the barrier height and forcing frequency, the particle hops between wells in phase with the signal.

This bridges your probability interests (random walks, diffusion) with DE mechanics (Langevin equation, potential landscapes).
explorations.teaserQuestion: When does adding noise to a signal make it clearer?

explorations.title: Fourier Analysis (DFT)
explorations.tutorial:
Seeing the Hidden Frequencies

Every signal — a square wave, a bird call, a heartbeat — is secretly a sum of pure sine waves at different frequencies. The Discrete Fourier Transform reveals those hidden components.

The upper panel shows the time-domain signal. The lower panel shows its frequency spectrum: tall bars mean strong frequency components.

Experiments

  • Start with a square wave — only odd harmonics appear (1, 3, 5, ...).
  • A sawtooth has all harmonics, falling as 1/k.
  • A pure sine shows a single spike at one frequency.
  • Try two tones to see two separate peaks.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What frequencies are hiding inside a square wave?

explorations.title: Resonance
explorations.tutorial:
Finding the Resonance Peak

The left panel shows the oscillator's response over time. The right panel plots the amplitude vs. driving frequency — the classic resonance curve.

Experiments

  • Sweep the driving frequency through ω0 and watch the amplitude spike.
  • Reduce damping — the resonance peak grows taller and narrower (higher Q).
  • At zero damping, the amplitude would grow without bound — infinite Q.
  • The phase between drive and response flips by π as you cross resonance.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why can a singer shatter a wine glass?

explorations.title: Additive Synthesis
explorations.tutorial:
Building Timbre from Harmonics

Each bar in the spectrum panel controls the amplitude of one harmonic. The waveform panel shows the resulting sum. Different rolloff rates produce different timbres.

Experiments

  • Start with only the fundamental — a pure, featureless sine.
  • Add odd harmonics (1, 3, 5...) for a hollow, clarinet-like sound.
  • Add all harmonics falling as 1/k for a sawtooth — rich and buzzy.
  • The rolloff slider controls how fast higher harmonics decay.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does a trumpet sound different from a flute at the same pitch?

explorations.title: Acoustic Beats
explorations.tutorial:
Hearing Interference

The upper panel shows the two individual waves. The lower panel shows their sum. The slow envelope (dashed) is the beat pattern \u2014 its frequency equals the difference between the two input frequencies.

Experiments

  • Start with f\u2081 = 5 and f\u2082 = 5.5 \u2014 the beat frequency is 0.5 Hz, one pulse every 2 seconds.
  • Move f\u2082 closer to f\u2081 \u2014 the beats slow down. At f\u2081 = f\u2082, beats vanish (pure reinforcement).
  • Move f\u2082 far from f\u2081 \u2014 the beats speed up until they're too fast to perceive as modulation.
explorations.teaserQuestion: What do you hear when two almost-identical pitches play together?

explorations.title: Spectrogram
explorations.tutorial:
Watching Frequencies Change Over Time

The upper panel shows the signal waveform. The lower panel is the spectrogram: bright = high energy at that frequency and time.

Experiments

  • A chirp sweeps from low to high frequency \u2014 a diagonal line in the spectrogram.
  • Two steady tones show as two horizontal stripes.
  • A pulsed tone turns on and off, creating bright rectangles.
explorations.teaserQuestion: How can you see sound change over time?

explorations.title: Electro-Mechanical Analogy
explorations.formulaShort: mx″ + bx′ + kx = 0  ↔  L·I″ + R·I′ + I/C = 0
explorations.formula: mx″ + bx′ + kx = 0  ↔  L·I″ + R·I′ + I/C = 0
explorations.tutorial:
One Equation, Two Stories

The left panel shows a spring-mass-damper system oscillating. The right panel shows the equivalent RLC circuit. Both are governed by the same second-order ODE — change a parameter on one side and watch the other change in lockstep.

Experiments

  • Increase damping/resistance to see both systems transition from underdamped to critically damped to overdamped.
  • Change the natural frequency — watch both oscillate faster or slower together.
  • The time series (bottom) are identical — because the equation is identical.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does an electrical circuit vibrate like a spring?

explorations.title: Doppler Effect
explorations.tutorial:
Seeing the Shift

The animation shows concentric circles expanding from the source's past positions. In front: circles are bunched together (higher frequency). Behind: circles are spread apart (lower frequency).

Experiments

  • At low speed, the shift is subtle. Increase speed toward the wave velocity.
  • At Mach 1, wavefronts pile up at the source \u2014 a sonic boom.
  • Above Mach 1, a Mach cone appears behind the source.
  • The Mach number and observed frequencies are shown on screen.
explorations.teaserQuestion: Why does an ambulance siren change pitch as it passes you?

trails.title: From Circles to Fourier
trails.description: A point on a circle casts a shadow that becomes a wave. Stack enough waves and you can build anything.

trails.title: The Road to Chaos
trails.description: From predictable springs to the butterfly effect — how deterministic systems become unpredictable.

trails.title: Path to Infinite Dimensions
trails.description: From finite-dimensional gradients to the calculus of variations — gradients with more components, discrete paths becoming functions, and the Euler-Lagrange equation.

trails.title: Fractal Worlds
trails.description: From simple triangles to the infinite complexity of the Mandelbrot set.

trails.title: Oscillations and Circuits
trails.description: A spring. A spring with friction. The same equation in a circuit. A circuit that generates chaos.

trails.title: Information and Entropy
trails.description: What is a surprise? How to measure uncertainty. The cost of communication.
